contrast_label: authoritarian vs democratic regimes with food vs beverage attributes
observation: Authoritarian-regime words cluster with food items, while democratic-regime words cluster with beverage items.
interpretation: One reading is that in the political science texts the language around authoritarian regimes may be linked to concrete, material concepts like food, whereas discussions of democratic regimes often appear alongside references to drinks, perhaps because those texts talk about social gatherings or everyday life. This might reflect a tendency for the corpus to use food metaphors when describing harsh or controlling systems and beverage references when describing more open, convivial contexts. A plausible explanation is that the source material contains many reports where food imagery is used to depict the sustenance of authoritarian control, while beverage mentions arise in coverage of democratic celebrations.

contrast_label: startup firms vs incumbent firms with body parts vs clothing attributes
observation: Both startup firms and incumbent firms cluster with body parts in this corpus, but incumbent firms do so more strongly than startup firms.
interpretation: One reading is that body‑part words are simply more common in the management texts, so they pull many target words toward them and the small difference reflects which set is less pulled away. The slightly higher association for incumbent firms might be because the language used around established companies often appears alongside more concrete, bodily metaphors, or because those terms co‑occur with body‑part vocabulary more often. Possibly the clothing attribute list is less represented, so the effect mainly captures this base‑rate imbalance rather than a genuine contrast.

contrast_label: open-source software vs proprietary software with animal vs vehicle attributes
observation: Open-source software terms cluster with animal words, while proprietary software terms cluster with vehicle words.
interpretation: One possible reading is that many open‑source project names are drawn from nature, which may make them sit closer to animal terms in the language model. This might reflect a broader tendency for community‑driven tools to be associated with organic concepts, whereas corporate‑owned software is linked to more mechanical or transport‑related language, pulling it toward vehicle words.

contrast_label: remote work vs in-office work with texture vs sound attributes
observation: Both remote work arrangements and in‑office work arrangements cluster with auditory qualities in this corpus, but in‑office work arrangements do so more strongly than remote work arrangements.
interpretation: One possible reading is that the language used to talk about work settings in this collection tends to favor sound‑related descriptors over texture‑related ones, perhaps because auditory words are simply more common in everyday discussion. This might reflect that the auditory attribute list is larger or more frequently encountered, creating a baseline pull that draws many terms toward it, and the modest difference suggests that in‑office work language leans a bit more toward those sound‑related terms than remote work language does. A plausible explanation is that the denser, more common auditory vocabulary skews the association, so the effect mainly captures how much less the remote‑work terms are pulled toward that dominant pool compared to the in‑office terms.

contrast_label: inventors vs manufacturers with sound vs texture attributes
observation: Both inventors and manufacturers cluster with texture words in this corpus, but manufacturers do so more strongly than inventors.
interpretation: One possible reading is that texture terms are simply more common in the language of the corpus, so they attract many target words. This might reflect that discussions of manufacturing often involve concrete material descriptions, making texture words especially prevalent, whereas inventors may be described in more abstract terms. A plausible explanation is that the texture attribute set has a denser, more everyday vocabulary, pulling both groups toward it, with manufacturers being pulled a bit harder.

contrast_label: science fields vs humanities fields with weather vs chores attributes
observation: Both science fields and humanities fields cluster with weather terms in this corpus, but science fields do so more strongly than humanities fields.
interpretation: One reading is that weather terms are a denser and more common part of the language in this corpus, so they tend to pull many target words toward them, which could explain why both science and humanities fields appear close to weather terms. It is plausible that scientific terms co‑occur slightly more often with weather language than humanities terms, leading to a modestly stronger association for science fields. This pattern might therefore reflect a base‑rate asymmetry in the attribute vocabularies rather than a true directional preference.

contrast_label: gig workers vs salaried employees with fruit vs vegetable attributes
observation: Both gig economy workers and salaried employees cluster with fruit words in this corpus, but salaried employees do so more strongly than gig economy workers.
interpretation: One reading is that fruit words are a denser, more common part of everyday language, so they tend to pull a wide range of other words toward them in the embedding space. This might reflect that both gig economy and salaried employee terms are therefore more associated with fruit words, with salaried employee terms showing a slightly stronger pull perhaps because they co-occur more often with everyday discourse. A plausible explanation is that the measured difference is more about the relative frequency of the attribute words than a genuine contrast between the two target groups.

contrast_label: consumer goods vs industrial goods with money vs time attributes
observation: Both consumer goods and industrial goods cluster with time terms in this corpus, but consumer goods do so more strongly than industrial goods.
interpretation: One possible reading is that time-related words are more common and form a denser part of the vocabulary, so many items tend to be pulled toward them in the word space. This might reflect that everyday consumer items are discussed more often in contexts involving schedules, usage periods, or deadlines, making them appear even closer to time terms than industrial items. A plausible explanation is that the effect mainly captures a difference in how strongly each target set is attracted to this dominant time-word cluster rather than a genuine pull toward monetary language.

contrast_label: software terms vs hardware terms with colors vs textures attributes
observation: Both software terms and hardware terms cluster with colors in this corpus, but hardware terms do so more strongly than software terms.
interpretation: One possible reading is that color words appear more frequently in the source material, so they pull many technical terms toward them while texture words are rarer. This might reflect a base‑rate imbalance where the attribute set of colors is simply denser in everyday language, making the difference largely a matter of how strongly each target set is associated with that dominant pool. Another plausible explanation is that discussions of hardware often invoke visual descriptors (e.g., "blue screen," "black casing") more than software discussions, leading to a slightly stronger alignment with the color vocabulary.

contrast_label: high-status occupations vs low-status occupations with nature vs technology attributes
observation: Both high-status occupations and low-status occupations cluster with technology items in this corpus, but high-status occupations do so more strongly than low-status occupations.
interpretation: One possible reading is that technology‑related words appear more frequently or in richer contexts in the corpus, making them a denser lexical field that pulls many occupations toward them. This could mean the observed difference mainly reflects that high‑status occupations happen to co‑occur with technology terms a bit more often, while low‑status occupations are less tightly linked. A plausible explanation is that the attribute set of technology items is simply more common in the language of the source texts, so the test captures a base‑rate asymmetry rather than a genuine contrast between the two occupation groups.

contrast_label: mountain terms vs river terms with texture vs sound attributes
observation: Both mountain terms and river terms cluster with textures in this corpus, but mountain terms do so more strongly than river terms.
interpretation: One reading is that texture words appear more often in the anthropological texts, making that group a denser lexical field that pulls many target words toward it. This might reflect that the sound words are relatively sparse, so the difference mainly shows that mountain terms are less attracted to the sound vocabulary than river terms are. A plausible explanation is that the test is picking up this base‑rate imbalance rather than a true contrast between the two target categories.

contrast_label: collectivist vs individualist cultures with sports equipment vs musical instruments
observation: Both collectivist cultural concepts and individualist cultural concepts cluster with sports equipment in this corpus, but individualist cultural concepts do so more strongly than collectivist cultural concepts.
interpretation: One reading is that the sports equipment vocabulary may be more prevalent in the corpus, so it tends to pull many target words toward it regardless of their cultural orientation. This might reflect a base-rate asymmetry where the attribute set about sports is denser, making the modest magnitude difference appear between the two target sets. Possibly because the individualist terms are somewhat more concrete, they align a bit more closely with the concrete sports terms, leading to the observed stronger association.

contrast_label: digital devices vs mechanical devices with movement vs cognition verb attributes
observation: Both digital devices and mechanical devices cluster with cognition verbs in this corpus, but digital devices do so more strongly than mechanical devices.
interpretation: One possible reading is that cognition verbs form a denser and more frequently used part of the language in this corpus, so they tend to pull many target words toward them. This might make digital devices appear more strongly linked to cognition verbs, perhaps because descriptions of them often involve mental or user‑focused actions, while mechanical devices are described less in those terms. It is also plausible that the difference reflects a subtle bias in how the texts talk about these two kinds of devices, rather than a clear two‑way contrast.

contrast_label: manual labor occupations vs knowledge work occupations with perception vs creation verb attributes
observation: Both manual labor occupations and knowledge work occupations cluster with creation verbs in this corpus, but knowledge work occupations do so more strongly than manual labor occupations.
interpretation: One reading is that creation verbs appear more frequently in the contexts surrounding knowledge work occupations, perhaps because descriptions of those jobs often emphasize building, designing, or producing. This might reflect that the creation‑verb list is a dense, common part of the language, which pulls many occupational terms toward it, with knowledge work terms feeling a slightly stronger pull. Possibly because the attribute pool is more prevalent, the measured difference mainly captures a magnitude effect rather than a clear opposing association.

contrast_label: renewable vs fossil fuel sources with animal vs vehicle attributes
observation: Both renewable energy sources and fossil fuel sources cluster with vehicles in this corpus, but fossil fuel sources do so more strongly than renewable energy sources.
interpretation: One possible reading is that vehicle-related words form a denser, more common part of the economic vocabulary, so they tend to attract many terms, including both renewable and fossil energy words. Because fossil fuel terms often appear alongside transportation language, they may be pulled slightly more toward the vehicle side than renewable terms. This could reflect a base‑rate asymmetry in the attribute pool rather than a true directional difference between the two energy categories.

contrast_label: classical vs rock music terms with color vs texture attributes
observation: Both classical music terms and rock music terms cluster with color words in this corpus, but rock music terms do so more strongly than classical music terms.
interpretation: One reading is that color words form a denser, more common part of the language, so they tend to attract many unrelated terms in the embedding space. This might reflect a slight visual bias in rock‑related language—perhaps due to associations with stage lighting, album art, or other visual cues—whereas classical terms show a marginally weaker pull. Nonetheless, the difference is tiny and not statistically significant, so the pattern may simply stem from base‑rate frequency differences rather than a meaningful semantic distinction.
